data.title: Bitcoin's Maginot Line: From Halving Failure to the Power Law Test
data.description:
I. A Phenomenon That Confuses Experienced Crypto Investors

The Bitcoin halving is the most classic story in the cryptocurrency world.

Every four years, miner rewards are halved, reducing the supply of new coins. The experience of the past three halvings shows that a spectacular bull market follows.

The 2012 halving resulted in a more than 90-fold increase. The 2016 halving resulted in a nearly 30-fold increase. The 2020 halving resulted in a more than 7-fold increase.

Then the halving will occur in April 2024.

From approximately $63,000 at the time of the halving to a high of $125,000 in October 2025, the increase was only 97%. Less than double. Alex Thorn, research director at Galaxy Digital, made a strong statement: the fourth cycle is dramatically weaker than previous ones. Upon seeing this, my first reaction was: has the old halving script stopped working? II. It's not just the increase that has decreased. If you look closely, it's not just the increase that has changed. Volatility has also decreased. In April 2020, Bitcoin's 30-day volatility once surged to 9.64%. In this round, however, that figure has never exceeded 3.11%. According to Bitbo data, it's currently only around 1.75%. The declines are also less pronounced. Previously, bear markets would easily see drops of 80% or 90%. This round, from $125,000 to $60,000, the drop is just over half. Zack Wainwright, a research analyst at Fidelity Digital Assets, has also noticed this change. He says that since institutional funds entered the market, it has indeed become more stable than before. Stability is a good thing, but it also means that the old strategy of blindly buying and waiting for profits to double may no longer work. III. What's Different About This Round? Many people attribute the smaller gains and lower volatility to the halving itself becoming ineffective. ChainChain believes things may not be that simple. The most direct reason is that this round had already seen a surge before the halving. In January 2024, the US approved the first batch of spot Bitcoin ETFs. This news directly pushed Bitcoin from over $40,000 to over $70,000 in March, setting a new all-time high at the time. The halving occurred in April. In other words, the positive impact was largely priced in before the halving even happened. This is the first time in history that a new high has been broken before a halving. In previous halvings, it took a long time to reach a new high after the halving. Therefore, if you start from $42,000 before the ETF was approved in January 2024 to the high of $125,000, the increase is approximately 197%. While this is still not as high as the 761% in 2020, it's at least better than 97%. However, no matter how the statistical methods are adjusted, the overall trend of decreasing price increases at each level remains unchanged. IV. A Deeper Question At this point, ChainChain wants to ask a deeper question: If the price increases decrease at each level and the price decreases also narrow at each level, does Bitcoin's cyclical pattern still exist? If it does, in what form will it manifest? There is a model that ChainChain has mentioned many times—the power-law model. This model states that Bitcoin's price does not grow exponentially, but rather exponentially. What does this mean? Exponential growth follows the sequence 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, doubling faster and faster. Power growth also follows the sequence 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, but the time required to double each time increases. Specifically for Bitcoin, the price roughly follows a time-to-the-5.7 power pattern. The lower band is the lower edge of this channel; historically, at the bottom of every bear market, the price has touched or even briefly fallen below it. This has been observed in the three bear markets of 2015, 2018, and 2022. Therefore, many people have formed the belief that the lower band is a solid bottom, and it's time to buy when it reaches that level. V. The Maginot Line's Warning But ChainTeaching recalls a piece of history. Before 2022, there was a similar belief in an unshakeable bottom. At the time, everyone said: Bitcoin's bear market bottom has never fallen below the historical high of the previous cycle. $20,000 is an impregnable bottom, as solid as a rock. And what happened? In June 2022, Bitcoin fell below $18,000. The unshakeable bottom was breached, and the belief collapsed. The Maginot Line tells a similar story. The French built an impregnable defensive line, but the Germans bypassed it through the Ardennes Forest and outflanked it without firing a single shot. The point of this discussion is: when everyone believes a certain line is impregnable, it is often its most vulnerable point. Will the power-law low-orbit repeat this pattern? 2026 may be the year the answer is revealed. VI. The Time Window of 2026 The power-law low-orbit is not static; it moves upwards every day. Based on its upward trend, the lower bound is projected to be approximately $52,000 in February 2026; $56,000 in April 2026 (currently); $65,000 by the end of October 2026; and $70,000 by the end of December 2026. The key window is in the fourth quarter of 2026. If the price falls to around $65,000 by then, it would be a textbook example of a bear market bottom—touching the power-law lower bound, and historical patterns would continue to hold true. If the price falls below $60,000, it will break through the lower trendline, and the model will fail for the first time. CryptoQuant analysts predict a solid bottom at $55,000-$60,000. This figure is precisely where the price would have fallen below the lower trendline. VII. Touching vs. Breaking Through: Completely Different Meanings Touching the lower trendline and breaking through it are two different things. Touching the lower trendline (around $65,000) has happened at the bottom of every bear market in history. The model allows for a brief dip; the key is whether it can recover. If it's just a touch and then a rebound, the power-law model remains valid. A drop below the lower bound (below $60,000) would mark the first time the price has broken through the model's lower limit. If the closing price remains below the lower bound for several consecutive weeks, the model is considered invalid. This suggests that Bitcoin's growth pattern may have changed—from a power law to another function? Santostasi, the proposer of the power-law model, emphasizes that the model should be falsifiable. If the price falls below the lower bound and persists for a period of time, the model should be disproven. This isn't the end of the world; it's scientific progress.

VIII. Has the Era of an 80% Deep Bear Market Been Over?

Returning to the initial topic of the halving.

To complete an 80% decline, the price needs to fall from $125,000 to $25,000. That would be a significant drop, breaking the lower limit of the power law, the 200-week moving average, and all historical support levels.

Based on current predictions from most institutions and most models, even the most pessimistic forecast is only $40,000-$50,000. Almost no serious analysis points to $25,000.

Is the era of an 80% deep bear market likely over? If the price falls to $65,000 in the fourth quarter of 2026, touching the lower bound, that would be a textbook bear market bottom. If it briefly falls below $60,000, that would be the twilight of the power-law model, but not the end of Bitcoin. 60% may be the limit of future bear markets. This is the price of asset maturity, and also a sign of asset maturity. IX. Some Thoughts on Bitcoin First, the halving did not fail, but its role has changed. Previously, the halving was a major engine of the bull market; now it's just one of many factors. Macroeconomic interest rates, ETF fund flows, and institutional allocation demand are becoming increasingly important. Secondly, the lower bound of the power law is not the Maginot Line. It may be broken, or it may not. But don't bet on it as an impregnable bottom. Thirdly, the history of the 2022 drop below the previous high of $20,000 tells us that all seemingly impregnable defenses are breached at the most unexpected times. Fourthly, models are tools, not beliefs. ChainChain will refer to power laws, but will not blindly follow them. Ultimately, the decision rests with one's own comprehensive judgment of the fundamentals, liquidity, and the macroeconomic environment. Fifth, regardless of whether it falls below the lower trendline, the era of 80% deep bear markets may be over. Bitcoin is transforming from a casino into an asset. While the excitement may be less, the stability is greater. Let's wait and see in the fourth quarter of 2026.

References:

[1] Vince Quill, "Current BTC Price Action Shows Dramatic Underperformance: Analyst", *Cointelegraph*, Apr 19, 2026. [Link](https://cointelegraph.com/news/bitcoin-cycle-dramatically-underperform-halving)

[2] Bitbo, "Bitcoin Volatility Index", accessed Apr 20, 2026. [Link](https://bitbo.io)

[3] Fidelity Digital Assets, research note on Bitcoin drawdowns and institutional impact, 2026.

[4] Giovanni Santostasi, "The Bitcoin Power Law", various analyses, 2020-2026.

[5] CryptoQuant, 2026 year-end Bitcoin bottom forecast.

[6] Jurrien Timmer, Fidelity Investments, research notes on Bitcoin key levels.

data.title: Predicting markets is not gambling, but a misunderstood "truth machine".
data.description:
Author: Jeff Park Source: X, @dgt10011 Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Last week, two media outlets, Axios and More Perfect US (MPU), respectively explained to the public what prediction markets are. Axios' Dan Primmark attempted to build a neutral platform, engaging in a heated debate with the founder of Kalshi (although his stance was quite biased); while MPU's Trevor Hayes was more direct, portraying prediction markets as a social ill.

Frankly, I partially agree with both sides' views. As someone who has worked for many years in the intersection of Wall Street and cryptocurrency, I understand people's growing unease about excessive financialization—a trend that is creating a social atmosphere of a "gambling-style public health crisis."

However, these media professionals often make the mistake of drawing conclusions first, then working backward to determine "who is fueling this phenomenon," ultimately distorting and confusing multiple different issues with overly simplistic narratives. One second they're discussing "insider trading," the next they're jumping to "online casinos," and finally attributing it all to "gambling addiction." This is precisely the core misunderstanding most people have about prediction markets: regardless of how you view the drawbacks of excessive financialization (whether it's zero-expiration options, swap ETFs, or trending stocks), prediction markets should be praised as tools for enhancing individual autonomy, uncovering the truth, and practicing decentralized ethical rights. Below, I will break down this issue more rationally. The Blurred Boundary Between "Investment" and "Gambling" The sole criterion for distinguishing between "investment" and "gambling" is whether the behavior generates a positive expected return (+EV), not whether the system itself is deterministic or random. In other words, the boundary is defined by the participants, not the game itself. Let's elaborate. In his MPU report, Trevor Hayes' questions always begin with "Obviously, predicting the market is gambling..." as if this were a self-evident conclusion. However, this fundamental premise precisely needs to be examined first. The biggest trend in the financial sector over the past two decades has been the increasingly blurred line between "investment" and "gambling": 60% of trading volume in the US stock market comes from high-frequency trading, and this is monopolized by oligopolies such as Simple Street and Citadel; Passive ETFs account for over 90% of total ETF assets under management (although active strategies are finally beginning to recover); The average holding period for US stocks has plummeted from nine years in the mid-1970s to only about six months in 2025. Meanwhile, the average daily trading volume in the US stock market has more than tripled over the past decade, also driven by algorithmic trading. Even more significant than these figures is the trend that retail investor trading volume exceeded $5 trillion in 2025, an increase of approximately 50% compared to 2023. However, you rarely see commentators accusing "stock trading of being gambling." Why? Because most people assume that stock picking is not gambling; it requires skill. This is a key understanding: labeling it "gambling" is unfair because it conflates skill-based games with purely probabilistic ones. For example, slot machines and poker are both called gambling, but they are fundamentally different—slot machines rely purely on luck with negative expected returns, while poker relies on real skill to achieve positive returns. To put it bluntly, the definition of "investment" versus "gambling" largely depends on whether people believe the strategy has positive returns, regardless of the "game" itself, whether it is deterministic (risk-free arbitrage, slot machines) or random (stock picking, poker). Like poker, prediction markets are random games with deterministic elements. Whether you consider it "gambling" or "investment" depends entirely on the participant—whether you are a highly autonomous, skilled individual, the opposite, or somewhere in between. This leads to the second question: if we consider gambling as participant-driven "speculation," how exactly do these markets operate? Where does the liquidity come from? The other side of speculation is insurance. All financial innovations initially resemble gambling. This was true of rampant insider trading in early stock markets, futures markets (where the Eurodollar was the first tool for government officials to conduct political insider trading), and modern commodity markets (where insider trading is almost impossible to define in the traditional sense). The reason is simple: the other side of speculation is insurance. These two aspects are two sides of the same coin. The essence of this kind of zero-sum game is the standardized transfer of synthetic risk. Moreover, not all "information" is naturally generated by private institutions. Critics of prediction markets often raise the question: "Some markets are purely speculative, unable to create social value, and shouldn't exist in the first place." The most typical example of this view is sports betting. Many people believe that sports belong to entertainment, and betting on entertainment is inherently unproductive. But this perception is wrong. Entertainment is social consumption, and can even be said to be one of the core sources of human happiness. More importantly, entertainment itself is economic consumption, possessing the characteristics of a two-sided market. The global sports industry generates over $50 billion in annual revenue, and including media, equipment, apparel, sports nutrition, and other related ecosystems, its scale is estimated to exceed $1 trillion. Take Nike as an example; it pays huge sponsorship fees to athletes and teams, and its capital allocation and risk hedging are naturally closely related to the results of events and the performance of players. The current societal perception of sports betting as equivalent to "casinos" stems solely from the lack of a formal market at the federal level, completely ignoring its untapped potential value. The value of derivatives lies in risk transfer, a core principle of all insurance models and asset securitization. The existence of an insurance market necessitates speculators as counterparties; in a transparent and open market, without government intervention, this is the only viable model. In fact, most insurance system failures arise from government intervention distorting the true market pricing of risk-takers. Insurance and asset securitization remain among the greatest financial innovations for improving capital efficiency. However, controversy remains: at what point does an event escalate from a normal financial service into a social ill? And how should we establish "event classification standards"? This is my final point. Two Key Characteristics that Distinguish Prediction Markets from Other Derivatives Prediction markets differ from other derivatives primarily in two aspects: **Precise Results** **Definitions with Clear Expiration Dates** To briefly review basic market-making logic: In most financial markets, the Central Limit Order Book (CLOB) is used to measure and provide liquidity because assets typically possess perpetual value. However, prediction markets are different: once the event outcome is determined, liquidity instantly drops to zero, and there are no more buy or sell orders. This is extremely unfriendly to liquidity providers—the binary 0 or 1 payout outcome completely invalidates the assumption of continuous dynamic hedging. More importantly, prediction markets are probability-based markets, not price-based markets. Contracts with odds around the 50% median have significantly higher liquidity than contracts with a 98% probability, because for the latter, a one percentage point change leads to an exponential increase in payout costs. In other words, liquidity cannot be sustained solely through price spreads, a point that fixed-income derivatives traders understand far better than stock traders (for example, a 10 basis point fluctuation at 4% and 0.5% interest rates has completely different implications). This means that in markets with severe information asymmetry and where outcomes can be precisely predicted, professional market makers are less willing to provide substantial liquidity. This also explains why the assumption that insiders "profit immensely" from information yields minimal gains in most scenarios. The market ultimately reflects what people truly care about. Even if I knew for sure whether "Jeff Parker will wear a Bitwise sweater in his next podcast," the market's liquidity would be essentially zero. Most arguments against insider trading assume that insiders can reap huge profits, but this is not the case. A market without value will not have natural liquidity; liquidity itself will accurately price the true value of information—and event classification standards will naturally form as a result. Why is the value of prediction markets far greater than their potential risks? As mentioned earlier, a major advantage of prediction markets is their accuracy, which is also their most valuable highlight. In today's over-financialized world, asset prices are determined more by technical factors and capital flows than by fundamental analysis and true value. Prediction markets, however, stand out by returning the purest "basis risk" to the facts themselves. In the future, if you predict that Tesla's revenue will exceed expectations, instead of buying stocks whose volatility is affected by external factors, you should bet on the prediction market; if you have unique insights into non-farm payroll data, you don't need to trade Eurodollar or mini S&P futures; you can directly bet on the data itself. In short, accuracy will truly reward excess returns, in-depth research, and genuine ability. Many people criticize prediction markets for preying on those lacking financial knowledge, assuming that "gamblers" will inevitably lose money, thus viewing them as a social ill. However, prediction markets actually possess the fairest mechanisms, providing positive returns for investors with independent judgment. More importantly, prediction markets have no "house"—unlike Las Vegas casinos that expel high-return players, prediction markets welcome truly capable individuals. Castle Securities and Charles Schwab have both announced plans to enter the prediction market. Are they "preying on the economically disadvantaged"? Clearly not. They understand better than most that speculation is insurance; your risk concavity is my return convexity. Why authoritative media fear the truth market. Finally, one more point. After reading the above, you will at least agree that prediction markets possess immense value if properly regulated. We can completely solve the "gambling problem" and "social ills" while ensuring the benefits outweigh the costs. But there's a crucial question we've overlooked: "What happens when insider trading occurs in markets that concern significant public interests? Could this become a tool for private profit?" This is a very complex question, which I will address in detail in another article. Here, I'd like to share a book I recently read—Ashley Linsberg's *The Gray Lady Winked*. The book documents decades of systemic lapses by *The New York Times*, which were by no means accidental: covering up the Stalinist famine, providing unusual coverage of Castro's rise, promoting Iraq's weapons of mass destruction, downplaying the danger of Hitler's rise to power… This authoritative media outlet has consistently relied on its information channels, ideology, and institutional self-preservation instincts to conceal the truth, create consensus, and then whitewash its mistakes afterward. This book helps us re-understand "media bias": it's not simply a left-right debate, but a structural problem of authoritative institutions creating consensus and whitewashing errors. Returning to the beginning: Axios and MPU are not neutral in this discussion either. In the future, you will see more and more media criticizing prediction markets, and their reasons for opposing them are precisely the reasons you should support them. Information has value, that's undeniable. I often say: the opposite of misinformation is not necessarily the truth, but rather state-controlled information. The real controversy lies in: who has the right to price information? Who profits from it? And does all of this occur before the public becomes aware of it? When insiders hoard asymmetric information, the motivation for money is far less important than the power struggle. Exploiting public ignorance, information can be weaponized to manipulate public opinion, spread misinformation, and ultimately control the market for truth. Therefore, the core of opposing insider trading is not economic efficiency, but the right to access information—some people trade based on what they know, while most people can only trade based on what they are allowed to know. Once you understand this, you won't be pessimistic about prediction markets; instead, you'll view the world more clearly and accurately. This is why I've always believed that being optimistic about prediction markets is one of the most democratic values.

data.title: Why do Strategy and BitMine have a continuous stream of funds to hoard cryptocurrencies?
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary

Strategy and BitMine both significantly increased their BTC and ETH holdings last week. STRC is Strategy's core tool for raising funds to buy BTC; equity financing and staking rewards have supported BitMine's continued ETH purchases.

Despite the current sluggish crypto market, both major crypto treasuries continue to increase their crypto asset holdings. Strategy spent $2.54 billion last week to purchase 34,164 Bitcoins, bringing its total holdings to 815,061 Bitcoins; BitMine added over 100,000 ETH last week, bringing its total holdings to over 4.97 million ETH.

How do Strategy and BitMine achieve their continuous buying plans?

I. Strategy Continues to Buy $2.54 Billion in BTC

According to an 8-K filing submitted to the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on April 20, Strategy acquired 34,164 bitcoins for $2.54 billion between April 13 and 19. This is Strategy's third-largest bitcoin acquisition ever, after the 55,500 BTC and 51,780 BTC purchased in November 2024. ...I. Strategy Continues to Buy $2.54 Billion in BTC

The average price of the newly purchased BTC was $74,395 per BTC, slightly lower than the company's average acquisition price of $75,527.

1. Why continue buying BTC?

In early April 2026, Strategy founder Michael Saylor publicly asserted that Bitcoin's "four-year halving cycle" had ended. He believes that the price is now driven by capital flows, rather than the supply shock brought by the halving.

Saylor's judgment on 2026 is particularly noteworthy, as he believes that 2026 will be the last year when people can buy Bitcoin for less than $100,000.

Saylor's judgment on 2026 is particularly noteworthy, as he believes that 2026 will be the last year that people can buy Bitcoin for less than $100,000.

Strategy's purchases have far exceeded BTC production, causing it to continuously deplete the circulating BTC supply. This supply-demand imbalance also puts Strategy in a position where the more it buys, the scarcer the BTC becomes, and the more valuable the company becomes. 2. Where does Strategy's money come from? Strategy's STRC provided over 85% of the funding for this acquisition. Similar to its recent acquisitions, the majority of funding for Strategy's latest acquisition came from STRC. According to the filings, STRC generated $2.18 billion in revenue, approximately 85.7% of total revenue, while the sale of Class A common stock (MSTR) contributed $366 million.

STRC, as a floating-rate perpetual preferred stock, is the core tool for Strategy to raise funds to buy BTC. It has a face value of $100 and a floating annualized dividend yield of 11.5% in April 2026, which will be adjusted monthly. It is primarily aimed at investors seeking stable cash flow and who are optimistic about the long-term trend of Bitcoin. Since its launch in July 2025, STRC has become a core support for Strategy's BTC accumulation, with a peak daily contribution of over 4,000 BTC and a peak weekly contribution of over 7,000 BTC, far exceeding the daily new mining supply. STRC's ex-dividend date passed last Wednesday, meaning investors who purchased the dividend product will no longer receive the next dividend payment. Prior to reaching this threshold, STRC's stock price remained at $100 or above for 10 consecutive trading days, with consistently strong demand for this $8.5 billion preferred stock. Andy Constan, founder of research firm Damped Spring Advisors, believes the surge in demand for STRC is primarily driven by dividend arbitrage traders, who typically buy the stock before the ex-dividend date and sell it shortly after. "Everyone I know went long STRC on leverage last night; they've never done dividend arbitrage in their lives."

II. BitMine Increases Holdings by Over 100,000 ETH

On April 20th, according to a BitMine report, the company's cryptocurrency holdings included 4,976,485 ETH at $2,301 each; 199 BTC; $200 million worth of Beast Industries shares; $107 million worth of Eightco Holdings shares; and a total of $1.12 billion in cash. Bitmine's Ethereum holdings represent 4.12% of the total Ethereum supply (120.7 million ETH).

1. Why Continue Buying ETH?

First, Bitmine Chairman Tom Lee believes the current bear market is about to end, so the company has accelerated its ETH purchases.

"We are seeing increasing signs that the 'mini-crypto' winter is coming to an end. With the downside risks of the Iraq War diminishing, ETH has risen 41% from its early February lows. Since the outbreak of the war, Ethereum has outperformed the S&P 500 by 2280 basis points and remains the best-performing single asset globally (excluding oil prices). In our view, Ethereum is the best 'wartime store of value' and has been the leading asset since the outbreak of the war, which is significant. Bitmine has continued to accelerate its ETH purchases over the past four weeks because our base case expectation is that ETH is in the final stages of the 'mini-crypto winter.' Last week, we purchased 101,627 ETH, the highest purchase rate since the week of December 15, 2025." Secondly, Ethereum continues to benefit from two major positive factors: Wall Street's tokenization of blockchains and the growing demand for public and neutral blockchains for intelligent agent AI systems. By 2026, public blockchains will become a necessity for autonomous AI agents to operate, and these agents require neutral and decentralized payment channels. By controlling 4.21% of the ETH supply, Bitmine effectively controls a significant portion of the "computing space" upon which these systems depend. 2. Where does Bitmine's money come from? BitMine's funding sources differ from Strategy's, primarily relying on equity financing and staking rewards. According to BitMine's 10-Q filing on April 14, the number of its common shares increased from 232 million shares between August 31 and February 28 to nearly 494 million shares. Additional paid-up capital jumped from $8.36 billion to $18.55 billion, all of which was invested in Ethereum. Bitmine also stakes a large portion of its ETH holdings to generate passive income. According to Bitmine's disclosure, it recently launched MAVAN (Made in the USA Validation Network)—an institutional-grade staking platform. A portion of Bitmine's ETH has been staked on the MAVAN platform. As of April 20th, Bitmine had a total of 3,334,637 ETH staked (equivalent to $7.7 billion at $2,301 per ETH). Tom Lee stated, "Bitmine has more ETH staked than any other institution in the world. When all of Bitmine's ETH is staked by MAVAN and its staking partners, it is expected to generate $330 million in ETH staking rewards annually (based on a 7-day BMNR yield of 2.88%). Currently, Bitmine's annualized staking yield is $221 million." In summary, BitMine raises funds by issuing new shares in the capital market and then stakes ETH to generate cash flow returns, thereby implementing a strategy of continuously buying and accumulating ETH.

data.title: Revolut Delays IPO Until 2028 As It Focuses On UK Banking Licence And US Expansion Plans
data.description:
Revolut is delaying its long-expected public listing as it doubles down on banking expansion in the UK and the United States, choosing to stay private for longer while scaling revenue, regulatory approvals, and global operations.

Chief executive Nik Storonsky now points to 2028 as the earliest possible IPO window, narrowing earlier expectations of a shorter timeline.

IPO Timeline Slips Towards 2028 As Revolut Stays Private Longer

The fintech group has effectively ruled out a near-term stock market debut, with Storonsky confirming the company is still “two years away” from an IPO.

That places any listing around 2028, as Revolut prioritises building its banking credentials over entering public markets.

He said the decision is not driven by urgency to raise capital but by long-term positioning.

In an interview with Bloomberg, he stated,

“We’re a bank, and for a bank, it’s super important to have trust. Public companies are trusted more compared to private companies.”

Revolut CEO Nik Storonsky just said the IPO is at least two years away (2028 at the earliest).

"We’re a bank, and for a bank, it’s super important to have trust. Public companies are trusted more compared to private companies,”— in his upcoming interview episode with David… pic.twitter.com/lSQYMAB4n7

— Max Karpis (@maxkarpis) April 20, 2026

The shift tightens earlier guidance of a two-to-three-year window and signals a deliberate strategy to remain private while expanding into full-scale banking operations.

Why Revolut Is Choosing Trust Over A Faster Listing

Storonsky’s approach reflects a broader change in how Revolut sees itself.

Once known primarily as a payments app, the company now operates across banking, lending, trading, crypto services and business finance.

The leadership view is that credibility with regulators and customers matters more than speed to market.

By 2022, Revolut had transformed from "useful travel app" to an entire financial universe:

• Banking/payments
• Trading/investments
• Insurance
• Travel perks
• Merchant services
• Rewards platform

The "super-app" strategy is working brilliantly: pic.twitter.com/xhgQ6PX3sC

— Ole Lehmann (@itsolelehmann) May 15, 2025

That is especially important as Revolut moves deeper into regulated banking in multiple jurisdictions.

Secondary share sales are being used to manage liquidity for employees and early investors, reducing pressure for a public listing while still allowing valuation growth.

Secondary Sales Keep Liquidity Flowing As Valuation Climbs

Revolut continues to rely on private market transactions to support shareholders before any IPO.

Its most recent secondary share sale in November valued the company at $75 billion, up from $45 billion a year earlier.

A further secondary deal is being considered for 2026, with reports suggesting it could push valuation beyond $100 billion.

Longer-term investor expectations place a potential IPO valuation at around $150 billion.

At the top end of projections, some incentive structures tied to Storonsky’s stake could activate if the company reaches a $200 billion valuation.

These private transactions have allowed Revolut to extend its runway without entering public markets, while maintaining strong investor interest.

Regulatory Approvals In UK And US Reshape Growth Path

A major milestone came in March 2026 when Revolut secured a full UK banking licence after a lengthy review by regulators.

The approval followed years of scrutiny over risk management and compliance systems, granting the company full banking status in its home market.

Revolut, with its UK banking licence, is sparking a "deposit war" that could hit the profits of NatWest and Lloyds.

Analysts predict Revolut's UK deposit base could surge from under £10B to £40B in the next 3/4 years.

Bloomberg analyst Tomasz Noetzel called the licence a… pic.twitter.com/iiWhyMZ3pF

— Max Karpis (@maxkarpis) March 20, 2026

At the same time, Revolut applied for a US national bank charter with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation.

Approval would give it direct access to Federal Reserve payment systems and enable it to expand lending and credit card services in the US.

And for our U.S. customers, a license will allow us to move faster and deliver more, including:

• Rapid product innovation: We’ll own the end-to-end experience ourselves.

• FDIC insurance: Customers’¹ deposits will continue to be insured by the FDIC.

• Real-time…

— Revolut (@Revolut) March 5, 2026

The company has also appointed senior leadership to drive its American operations, signalling a stronger push into one of its most important growth markets.

Revenue Growth And Profitability Strengthen Private Position

Revolut’s financial performance has improved significantly during its extended private phase.

It reported around $4 billion in revenue in 2024, rising to approximately $6 billion in 2025.

Profitability has also accelerated, with pre-tax profit increasing from $1.4 billion in 2024 to about $2.3 billion in 2025, marking five consecutive years of profit.

The company has projected revenue of roughly $9 billion for 2026, alongside continued margin strength.

Transaction volumes underline its scale, with around $1.7 trillion processed in 2025 across payments, FX, trading, and business services.

UK Fintech brand @Revolut has emerged as the fastest growing among the world’s 500 most valuable brands this year. In 2026, Revolut’s brand value has more than tripled, up 239% to USD6.6 billion from USD1.9 billion in 2025, marking its debut into the Global 500 2026.

This surge… pic.twitter.com/1w50d7n85M

— Brand Finance (@BrandFinance) January 22, 2026

Crypto Trading And Banking Expansion Drive User Growth

Revolut has grown to serve around 70 million customers across more than 40 markets. Its platform has expanded far beyond foreign exchange and payments into a broader financial ecosystem.

It now offers current accounts, business banking, stock trading, lending, eSIM mobile services, and access to more than 300 crypto tokens, positioning it among the more crypto-integrated banking platforms in Europe.

The trading function operates through external infrastructure, with execution, pricing, and clearing handled by CMC Connect, the institutional arm of CMC Markets, while Revolut manages the customer interface.

How Revolut’s B2B Push Adds A New Revenue Layer

Beyond retail users, Revolut Business has become a growing part of its income base, generating over $500 million annually, or roughly 16 percent of group revenue.

Revolut Leans Heavily on Business Banking: "We’re Growing Even Faster on Our Business Product"

Revolut is rapidly expanding its business banking offering, which is now growing faster than its consumer product and has become a major global revenue driver.

Antoine Le Nel, Revolut… pic.twitter.com/KKbDanZ0hN

— Max Karpis (@maxkarpis) April 15, 2026

The company recently launched Revolut BillPay, a supplier payments platform covering more than 150 jurisdictions, expanding its treasury and FX services for corporate clients.

More than ten product lines now each generate over $100 million annually, showing a widening spread of revenue sources beyond core consumer banking.

Can Revolut Outpace Traditional And Digital Rivals?

Revolut’s scale and multi-market banking progress place it in a different bracket from many European fintech competitors such as Monzo, Starling, N26 and Chime, many of which remain smaller or unprofitable.

Its combination of banking licences, international expansion, and high transaction volumes has strengthened its position as one of the larger private fintech groups globally.

With regulatory approvals progressing in both the UK and US, and profitability continuing to rise, the company appears focused on building institutional trust and operational depth before entering public markets.

data.title: The rise of intelligent agents has changed the entire distribution of the AI ​​value chain.
data.description:
The main narrative of AI investment is undergoing a structural shift. A recent study by Morgan Stanley points out that as AI moves from "generating content" to "automating tasks," the next round of incremental growth in AI infrastructure will expand from a "single-chip computing power race" to "full-stack systems engineering"—GPUs will remain core, but will no longer exclusively enjoy budgets and premiums. According to the trading platform Zhuifeng, Morgan Stanley research analyst Shawn Kim wrote directly in a report, "Agent AI marks a structural shift from computation to orchestration." In agent workflows, CPU-side orchestration time can account for 50% to 90% of total latency, leading to an estimated $32.5 billion to $60 billion in incremental CPU market space by 2030, pushing the total TAM (Total Assets) for server CPUs to the $82.5 billion to $110 billion range. Meanwhile, DRAM, ABF substrates, wafer foundry, memory, connectors, and passive components will all rise from supporting roles to become new bottlenecks and profit centers. This will generate an additional 15 to 45 EB of DRAM demand by 2030, equivalent to 26% to 77% of the industry's annual supply in 2027. This assessment means to the market that the beneficiaries of AI capital expenditure will expand from a few chip giants to the entire global supply chain. The next round of excess profits may come more from the "enabling links" that first become bottlenecks in the intelligent agent workflow and are most difficult to scale up quickly. As bottlenecks shift across different links, the weight distribution of the AI ​​value chain changes accordingly. From "Generation" to "Action": Intelligent Agents Push the Bottleneck from Computing Power to Orchestration The typical workflow structure of generative AI is relatively simple: after a user request arrives, the CPU performs a small amount of preprocessing, the GPU is responsible for token generation, and then the result is returned. Throughout the entire chain, the GPU is the absolute protagonist, while the CPU only undertakes auxiliary functions. The operating logic of intelligent agents is completely different. To complete a task, the system needs to go through multiple steps such as planning, retrieval, calling external tools and APIs, execution, reflection and iteration, and also involves a large number of "control plane" capabilities such as multi-agent collaboration, permission management, state persistence, and scheduling. Morgan Stanley's core conclusion is that intelligent agents do not bring more "heavy" single inference, but rather more steps, more states, and more coordination, and these tasks are naturally more suitable for CPU processing. This leads to two direct consequences: first, the CPU-to-GPU ratio at the cluster level will systematically increase; second, DRAM will be upgraded from a "capacity configuration item" to a "core system component for performance and throughput." Bottlenecks in data centers will increasingly appear in memory bandwidth, data transfer, interconnect latency, and system-level coordination, rather than simply GPU computing power. The CPU ratio is being reassessed: from "1:12" to "1:2" and even reversed. In the past, "one CPU serving approximately 12 GPUs" was a typical architectural description of AI servers. However, the report points out that as agent workflows become longer and tool calls and context management become more complex, this ratio is rapidly narrowing. Taking NVIDIA's roadmap as an example, updated estimates show that near the Rubin platform, the CPU to GPU ratio is already close to 1:2; if it evolves towards more aggressive forms like Rubin Ultra, it may even see a reverse configuration of 2 CPUs to 1 GPU. Even if it only improves from 1:12 to 1:8, the absolute demand for CPUs will jump by an order of magnitude for ultra-large-scale deployments. Once this direction is established, the demand elasticity for CPUs will shift from "following server shipments" to "following agent complexity," meaning that the growth in CPU demand will be more structural, rather than simply a continuation of the traditional hardware replacement cycle.

CPU TAM Recalculation: $82.5 Billion - $110 Billion by 2030, Increment from Orchestration

Morgan Stanley adopts a "system layering" approach, separating the CPU opportunities brought by intelligent agents from the traditional server upgrade logic, and establishing three independent analytical perspectives:

    Wafer foundry (especially advanced processes):The CPU foundry market is projected to reach approximately $33 billion in 2026 and $37 billion in 2028. TSMC's share in the CPU foundry market is expected to further increase from approximately 70% in 2026 to approximately 75% in 2028; and Intel is expected to begin outsourcing server CPUs to TSMC in the second half of 2027. ...> BMC and Memory Interface: Aspeed is highlighted as a core beneficiary of CPU server BMC, holding approximately 70% market share in this segment. The new generation AST2700 platform brings a 40% to 50% increase in ASP. Montage is placed in the "memory interconnect" value chain, with a global revenue share of approximately 36.8%. CPU Socket and Passive Components: The report uses Lotes and FIT as direct mappings to CPU sockets, estimating that for every additional 1 million CPUs required, Lotes revenue increases by approximately 0.6%, and FIT revenue increases by approximately 0.2% (based on socket metrics only). Regarding passive components, using the simplified assumption of "approximately $30 worth of MLCC content per general-purpose server," an additional $500 million in MLCC demand is projected for 2030, representing about 2% to 3% of the global MLCC market at that time. CPUs represent the clearest growth driver, but the "enabling segment" is more favored. The report acknowledges that the growth in agent workloads will structurally benefit AMD's cloud market share, but maintains an equal-weight rating for both AMD and Intel. It prefers to track the agent theme through companies like NVIDIA and Broadcom, where "capital expenditure and token growth are more directly mapped to profitability," while also listing valuation constraints as an important consideration. From a broader perspective, the core value of this report lies in upgrading the investment paradigm for AI from a "single-point computing power arms race" to "system efficiency and bottleneck economics": GPUs are the engine, CPUs are the transmission and control system, and memory and interconnects are the fuel system and chassis—single-point excellence remains important, but the key to scalable returns is the synergy of the entire system. For the industry chain, this means that the excess returns from AI investment will be more diversified and longer-term: not only from the "most powerful GPUs," but also from those links that first become bottlenecks in the intelligent agent workflow and are most difficult to scale up quickly. High-frequency verification indicators that can be continuously tracked include: the upward revision of CPU quantity and memory configuration in the new platform BOM, the pace of long-term agreement signings with cloud vendors, and the utilization trend of ABF carrier boards and advanced process capacity.

data.title: The Tokenized Goods Market in 2026: Evolution, Applications, and Outlook
data.description:
Author: Jinming, Research Assistant at HashKey Capital; Translated by Shaw Jinse Finance

Overview

Overview: The tokenized commodities market has seen rapid expansion over the past year, with assets under management (AUM) growing from $1.37 billion to $5.46 billion, an annual growth rate of approximately 300%. While growth in this sector continues to accelerate, this expansion is primarily driven by rising demand for precious metals, especially gold. The two leading tokenized gold assets—Tether's XAUT and Paxos' PAXG—together account for approximately 95% of the total tokenized commodities market.

This concentration itself is not a weakness of the industry, but rather the clearest signal that such assets are achieving product-market fit on-chain, which is precisely what has driven its recent growth. Now, the core issue for the industry is no longer whether tokenized commodities can continue to develop, but when they will become mainstream assets recognized by both retail and institutional investors. This article will analyze the growth trajectory of tokenized commodities, the underlying logic supporting this trend's long-term continuation, and define the challenges and opportunities for its next stage of development. Tokenized Commodity Market Size Data From 2025 to the present, the tokenized commodity market size has grown from $1.37 billion to $5.3 billion in the first quarter of 2026, an increase of over 530%. From 2025 to the first quarter of 2026, the sector saw continuous monthly net inflows of funds, highlighting the sustained market demand for tokenized commodities and the increasing participation of institutional investors. Tokenized commodities can be categorized into several major types: precious metals, energy, agricultural products, industrial metals, and livestock products. Development across these categories is uneven. Driven by gold, tokenized precious metal products account for over 95% of the market share, far exceeding other categories. This phenomenon is not surprising: gold prices surged in 2025, outperforming other assets; coupled with escalating global geopolitical tensions, central banks and institutions worldwide accelerated their gold reserve purchases. Nevertheless, this initial market enthusiasm confirms the market suitability and distribution potential of tokenized commodities. We believe this growth trend will continue as more asset classes are gradually launched.

Comparison of Commodity Performance with Bitcoin and Ethereum in 2025

Source: Bloomberg, CoinMarketCap

Growth Trajectory of Tokenized Commodities

Source: rwa.xyz, Allium

Will tokenized commodities exist in the long term?

Yes!

Several structural factors determine that this is a long-term trend, not a cyclical market: On-chain portfolio diversification provides new price discovery channels in an environment where traditional financial markets have limitations. It opens up investment channels to the public, allowing directional bets on single assets, which are often harder to obtain than indices. Increasing institutional adoption will drive scale expansion and channel penetration. DeFi composability fosters new use cases, such as collateralized lending. On-chain portfolio diversification... Data shows that commodities have extremely low correlation with stocks, crypto assets, fixed-income products, and the US dollar. Furthermore, commodities possess anti-inflationary properties. Although Bitcoin is touted as a safe-haven asset, its price movements are volatile, behaving more like risk assets. Including tokenized commodities in a portfolio can help achieve diversification and mitigate market volatility.

Source: Yahoo Finance

Price Discovery Function

Traditional derivatives markets close major exchanges, such as the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME), on weekends. This is a structural weakness for institutions managing global portfolios across Asia and the US time zone, or any participant who needs to hedge risk exposure during weekend macroeconomic events, and its drawbacks become glaringly apparent at critical moments.

Source: Allium

Mass Access to Investment Channels

For retail traders, especially those in emerging markets, participating in commodity trading is almost an unattainable dream—these markets have traditionally been open only to institutions. Even in developed markets, the categories available to retail investors are mostly limited to precious metals widely traded on exchanges, such as gold and silver.

Source: Etherscan

The table below lists an incomplete list of institutional adoptions.

DeFi's composability fosters innovative on-chain use cases

A more structurally significant development is that these assets are beginning to function as interest-bearing financial instruments within the decentralized finance (DeFi) system, a function that remains highly limited in traditional financial markets.

Tether's gold token (XAUT) has been used as collateral on the Aave platform to lend stablecoins and participate in on-chain yield strategies; it can also be traded on decentralized exchanges such as Uniswap or used as a reserve asset. As decentralized finance continues to mature, commodity tokens will transform from simple stores of idle value into productive assets with yield potential.

DeFi Protocol Adoption of XAUT

Source: Etherscan

Tokenized Commodities on Public Chains

Ethereum, with its mature decentralized finance (DeFi) and stablecoin ecosystem, has become the core channel for the issuance and circulation of tokenized commodities, allowing issuers to easily leverage network effects to expand their business.

However, institutions are continuously advancing their multi-chain deployments, a strategy that may reshape the landscape of various chains in the future. Arbitrum's growth is the most prominent: its related asset size was almost negligible at the beginning of 2025, but climbed to $85 million by the end of 2025. In 2026, Arowana, a gold tokenization platform backed by South Korea's Hanwha Group, officially launched, leveraging Hanwha's existing gold exchange and fully compliant infrastructure to offer tokens backed by physical gold to retail investors. This move is significant: as South Korea's third-largest gold exchange operator, Hanwha Group has a trading volume of $600 million and is now advancing its digital commodities strategy through permissionless blockchain, which will effectively boost market liquidity and is a major boon to Arbitrum's Real-World Asset (RWA) ecosystem. Polygon and XRP Ledger have also been selected as issuance vehicles for tokenized commodities, but on-chain DeFi-related activities remain relatively limited.

Source: rwa.xyz

Regional Adoption of Tokenized Commodities

By region, Singapore and Hong Kong are currently more active in tokenization infrastructure construction, compliant issuance, and exchange access for tokenized commodities; Japan and South Korea are more active in on-chain asset issuance led by issuers or initiators. The United States is extremely active in issuance, custody, and channel distribution.

The EU stands out in the areas of tokenized infrastructure and custody solutions. Challenges: While the long-term structural growth trend of tokenized commodities remains unchanged, growth across different categories will continue to be uneven, mainly constrained by the following factors: Exchange Listing Difficulty: Most tokenized commodities are not yet listed on mainstream centralized exchanges, limiting channel expansion. However, as regulatory policies become clearer, introducing high-demand tokenized commodities will be a natural choice for exchanges. Binance, the world's largest exchange, recently took the lead, announcing its first support for the gold token XAUT.

  • Low Liquidity: XAUT and PAXG enjoy good liquidity due to institutional trust and channel advantages, while other tokenized assets are struggling, with generally insufficient liquidity across protocols, leading to high slippage costs for holders. This issue is gradually improving as several market makers, including Flow Traders, Wintermute, FalconX, B2C2, and GCEX, announce access to tokenized commodities for professional and institutional clients.

  • Preference for Physical Redemption: Retail investors hoping to redeem physical commodities may be disappointed. Asset tokenization does not equate to direct redemption of the underlying physical commodity, as commodities and raw materials are strictly regulated. Redemption typically has a minimum threshold, which is often too high for retail investors. For example, Tether requires a minimum physical redemption of 340 troy ounces of gold, equivalent to one gold bar; on platforms like metals.io, only compliant institutions can redeem uranium, gold is only redeemable in multiples of 1 kilogram, and strategic metals require redemption through compliant accounts with a minimum value of $10,000. Regulatory Obstacles: Prohibiting retail investors from trading tokenized commodities will hinder their future adoption. Depending on the structure and marketing methods of tokenized commodities, they may be classified as commodities or securities in different jurisdictions, requiring careful adherence to compliance standards. DeFi Integration Issues: Some DeFi application scenarios may complicate the anchoring relationship between token prices and commodity spot prices. Auditing and Transparency: Blockchain can verify the authenticity and circulation of tokens on-chain, but it cannot control the physical management of commodities. Many commodity categories have different grading standards, requiring a comprehensive legal framework, insurance, regular physical audits, and on-chain verification to ensure transparency and credibility. Future Outlook: Current institutional applications of tokenized commodities—primarily for self-custody and portfolio hedging—are only the beginning of a long-term trend. A more decisive shift will be the transformation of these assets from passive stores of value to productive assets, supported by the programmability of blockchain. As DeFi liquidity deepens and more issuers pursue similar integrations, the on-chain commodity market will significantly differ from the traditional commodity market. The entry of institutional liquidity will also bring widespread channel effects and credibility, driving native crypto retail investors to participate in commodity investment. Short-term development will remain uneven, with precious metals being the biggest beneficiaries due to the expanding DeFi channels and higher market awareness. Energy and agricultural products have already achieved initial on-chain deployment, and in the future, with clearer regulations, increased market awareness, deeper DeFi integration, and wider application, large-scale on-chain circulation will eventually be achieved.

data.title: Warsh's nomination hearing testimony reveals his commitment to ensuring strict independence of monetary policy.
data.description:
Note: Federal Reserve nominee Warsh will attend his nomination hearing before the Senate Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee at 10 PM Beijing time on April 21. Politico released Warsh's testimony early this morning, which Claw of Jinse Finance has translated as follows:

Good morning, Mr. Chairman, and thank you all. It is an honor to be here today, and I extend my greetings to Senior Representative Warren and the entire committee. Thank you for taking the time to consider my nomination, and thank you for your support before and after my nomination.

I sincerely thank President Trump for entrusting me with this public responsibility. He firmly believes that the U.S. economy and people's real income will accelerate, and I share the President's confidence in this country and the American people. The potential for U.S. economic growth is rising.

Among those present today are some of my closest and longest-standing friends. I am especially pleased that my wife, Jane, is also here. At important moments in my life, I always think of my late parents. I am proud of them, and I hope they will be proud of me today.

We first reach a broad consensus: this is a pivotal moment for the national economy, perhaps the most critical turning point in generations. If policymakers across government departments respond to this crucial juncture with wisdom and clarity, the American economy will thrive. As a former Federal Reserve Governor and a friend and colleague of the past five Federal Reserve Chairs, I have a particularly clear understanding of the challenges and opportunities facing the Federal Reserve, which I cherish. To the President, Congress, and the American people, I pledge to do my utmost, with the utmost prudent judgment, to faithfully fulfill the mission entrusted to the Federal Reserve by Congress, including price stability and full employment. The American people expect the Federal Reserve to deliver on its promises. Members may be familiar with my educational background and professional experience; however, what truly shapes me are my more personal experiences and the people I have worked with and benefited greatly from. I completed high school in upstate New York, where I met many excellent teachers and made outstanding classmates whom I still remember vividly. It is truly fortunate to have the influence of good teachers and friends at the beginning of one's education and character development. Public education gave me this, and I am deeply grateful. Later, at Stanford University, as a student and researcher, I had the privilege of working with a group of accomplished economists and policymakers. Many of my mentors served in government and related institutions during a pivotal period in American history—the stagflation of the 1970s and the recovery of the 1980s and 90s. Former Secretary of State and Treasury George Shultz, a great patriot at the Hoover Institution, was someone I was fortunate to consider a mentor and close friend. That formative period was perfect: I learned rigorous thinking, mastered strict statistical and economic analysis methods, understood geopolitical and economic history, maintained independent thinking, resisted trends and groupthink, witnessed the humility of top experts, and most importantly, was surrounded by people wholeheartedly dedicated to American ideals and beliefs. The early 1990s in Silicon Valley provided the perfect backdrop for all of this. America was entering a new era of technology-driven innovation, and a new generation of entrepreneurs was emerging. Many of them were my classmates and later became lifelong friends. I don't know if it was all just a coincidence, but I was in the right place at the right time. These early influences set the standards I've always strived to uphold in my work in the public and private sectors. The same is true of my colleagues and mentors later in life. Over the past 15 years, I've gained solid practical experience in macroeconomics and financial markets, and I've had the privilege of working with one of the most successful investors of our time—Stan Druckenmiller. Stan never held a government position, yet he was a patriot. He didn't have a PhD, but I believe he was one of the best and most open-minded economic thinkers. He never publicized his philanthropy, yet he helped thousands of young Americans receive a top-notch education and real opportunities for upward mobility. Like Secretary Schultz, Stan never sat down to lecture me formally, but gave me something far more valuable: the opportunity to sit beside him and participate in decision-making. Without their guidance, and without other mentors at the Hoover Institution—including my current boss, close friend, and former Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice—I probably wouldn't be standing here today as the president's nominee for Chair of the Federal Reserve. But I can say one thing for sure: I wouldn't be so well prepared for this urgent and crucial task at hand. Between these important experiences, I spent over a decade in government, initially on the White House economic staff, and later as a member of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors. In fact, almost exactly 20 years ago, I testified before this committee as a nominee for the Federal Reserve Board of Governors. At that time, none of us—including myself—could have imagined that it would be such an unprecedented and extraordinary period. During the global financial crisis, with the economy reeling, unemployment soaring, the financial system teetering on the brink of collapse, and the United States' international standing severely tested, central banks played an indispensable role. My colleagues and I utilized policy tools and powers unique to the Federal Reserve, and also benefited greatly from the credibility built and passed down by our predecessors. In this exceptionally challenging situation, I witnessed the Federal Reserve and its staff at their best. I worked with dozens of outstanding and dedicated professionals in Washington and across the Federal Reserve Banks, united in a shared mission, under the wise and resolute leadership of Chairman Ben Bernanke. We worked closely with the Treasury, the government, and Congress to mitigate the risk of systemic collapse—a outcome that was not inevitable at the time. In the post-crisis period, I also saw the institution attempt to play a broader role in the economy and society, constantly expanding its authority and overdrawing its hard-won credibility, often, even with good intentions, touching or exceeding the statutory boundaries of the Federal Reserve's responsibilities. The role and responsibilities of central banks in a republic have existed since the founding of the United States; the heated debate surrounding central bank independence also has a long history. Therefore, I state unequivocally: Monetary policy independence is paramount. Monetary policymakers must act in the national interest, and decisions must be based on rigorous analysis, thorough deliberation, and clear judgment. I do not believe that the opinions of elected officials—the president, senators, and representatives—on interest rates pose a substantial threat to the operational independence of monetary policy. Central bank officials must be firm enough to listen to diverse opinions; humble enough to embrace new perspectives and economic dynamics; wise enough to translate incomplete data into valuable judgments; and responsible enough to make decisions faithfully and wisely. In short, the independence of the Federal Reserve largely depends on the Federal Reserve itself. This has three important implications: First, Congress entrusts the Federal Reserve with the mission of stabilizing prices, a mission that cannot be shirked, ambiguous, disputed, or obfuscated. Inflation is a choice, and the Federal Reserve must bear the responsibility for it. Low inflation is the Federal Reserve's "core shield," a key guarantee against various risks. Therefore, when inflation soars as it has in recent years, the American people, especially the most vulnerable groups, will suffer severely: declining purchasing power, lower living standards, and potentially even a loss of confidence in the economic governance system, leading to questioning the value of monetary policy independence. Second, while the Federal Reserve possesses the highest degree of independence in monetary policy operations, this independence does not cover all functions authorized by Congress. Federal Reserve officials do not enjoy equal special priority in areas such as public funds management, bank supervision policy, and international financial affairs. Third, the Federal Reserve must adhere to the boundaries of its responsibilities. Once it oversteps its bounds and venturing into fiscal and social policy areas where it has neither the authority nor the expertise, the Federal Reserve's independence will face the greatest risk. The Federal Reserve should not act as an "omnipotent institution" of the U.S. government, nor should it become an "appellate court" for matters that should be debated and decided by other departments. The Fed Chair may sometimes wish to have final say, but this is not how a republican system of government operates. I advocate for a clearer and more aligned division of powers and responsibilities within the Fed. During my time at the Fed, I stated, "Central bank officials must prove themselves capable of fulfilling their responsibilities and steadfastly upholding the institution's credibility. This means respecting our important but limited role in policymaking and fulfilling our mission with professionalism and consistency." This view remains unchanged. In conclusion, I believe that monetary policy independence is earned by avoiding interference, and this is how better decisions are made. I am committed to ensuring the strict independence of monetary policy operations, and equally committed to collaborating with the government and Congress on non-monetary policy matters within the Fed's purview. I pledge full responsibility for all the functions of the Federal Reserve. A quote from Milton Friedman, which I have always kept in mind since my student days, is the tyranny of the status quo. Anyone who has worked in a large, complex institution understands what this means: the pull of inertia, the acceptance of old patterns, the unwillingness to re-examine long-held assumptions, the use of outdated models that are no longer applicable, and procrastination. In today's rapidly changing world, clinging to old practices and policies is especially harmful. If confirmed as Chair, I will serve with the experience of a participant and the skepticism of an observer, ensuring that the Federal Reserve always remembers its boundaries, focuses on its core mission, and fulfills its statutory duties. I will be loyal to the Constitution, the Federal Reserve Act, and the best traditions of the Federal Reserve. I am familiar with this "battlefield" and will be honored to serve again on the Federal Reserve Board. At what is arguably the most critical juncture in American history, a reform-oriented Federal Reserve can bring tangible change to the American people. The risks we face are higher than ever before. Both within and outside the government, I am committed to finding common goals and working with colleagues in an open and collaborative manner. If confirmed, I will strive to create an environment where talented individuals can achieve their best career goals. Openness and goodwill are essential in pursuing our common goals, and I believe this hearing will test that. It is a great honor to stand before you all. Thank you, and I look forward to your questions.
